Title: Division 7A Loans
Content:
Division 7A contains anti-avoidance provisions designed to prevent tax-free payments from private companies to associated persons i.e. "debit loans". The effect of Division 7A is to deem these amounts as unfranked dividends (i.e. dividends with no imputation credits). The shareholder or their associated would need to include the full amount of the loan in their tax return.

This Company has entered into a Loan Agreement with the Trust Name for the repayment of their "debit loan" account in 202X. We have adjusted your 202X Financial Statements and Income Tax Returns to ensure that the appropriate interest and capital repayments have been made on your "debit loan".

DATEDESCRIPTIONREPAYMENTINTERESTBALANCE
*date*Description$0$0$0
$0$0$0

The loan will not be taken to be a dividend because the total repayments are greater than the minimum year repayment required for 202X.

During the 202X financial year, an additional $Amount was withdrawn from the company without income tax being paid. Therefore, a new loan agreement has been prepared to repay interest and principal amounts back to the business over a period of 7 years.

Under the terms of the Loan Agreement, the following minimum repayments are required during the 202X financial year.

Title: Division 7A - Debit Loan Warnings
Content:
In the Financial Statements of the Company, we believe that the following amounts would be viewed by the ATO as “debit loan accounts”. This represents cash that has been drawn out of the Company by individuals (or associated Trusts) without personal income tax being paid. GIVE AN EXPLANATION - eg. The main contributor to this situation was the use of business funds to purchase your investment property.

DETAILSAMOUNT
LOANS - name$0
TOTAL$0

The effect of Division 7A on the above loan accounts is to deem these amounts as unfranked dividends (i.e. dividends with no imputation credits) paid from the Company to shareholders. This could result in a bad tax outcome, with shareholders possibly paying tax at the top marginal rate of 47%.

Option 1 — Payment of Franked Dividend

You could issue a franked dividend from your Company to remove this loan account. For example, as at 30 June 2024 this Company had $amount in retained profits (please refer to the attached Financial Statements). This means that a franked dividend could be issued as follows:

Dividend - Person 1$amount
Dividend - Person 2$amount
Total$amount

Your debit loan accounts would then reduce to $NIL. The estimated extra income tax to pay in your 2024 Income Tax Return would be approximately $Extra Tax.

The above tax calculation assumes gross wages of approximately $Wages Amount from Business Name.

Option 2 — Formalise the Loans through a Loan Agreement

The above loans could be formalised through signing a Loan Agreement to repay interest and principal amounts back to the business. As they would be considered to be unsecured, they would need to be paid back over a 7-year period. The interest rate for the 2024 financial year is 8.27%. The minimum repayments are calculated under the relevant Tax Act provisions. This interest would not be tax deductible as it is not linked to any direct income producing investment.

How to Avoid This Situation in the Future

We recommend that you stop (or reduce) the use of company funds for personal items. You could also increase your wages to match the funds needed personally.

Title: Division 7A Loans & Distributable Surplus
Content:
In the Financial Statements of the Company, we believe that the director loans of $xxx would be viewed by the ATO as "debit loan accounts". This represents cash that has been drawn out of the Company by individuals (or associated Trusts) without personal income tax being paid.

The effect of Division 7A on the above loan accounts is to deem these amounts as unfranked dividends (i.e. dividends with no imputation credits) paid from the Company to shareholders. This could result in a bad tax outcome, with shareholders possibly paying tax at the top marginal rate of 47%.

Ordinarily, a dividend or injection of funds would be required to satisfy the Division 7A requirements.

Distributable Surplus

Upon further analysis of the company, we noted the following in relation to your distributable surplus:

  • the distributable surplus is nil.
  • the total dividends is nil.

The total of all deemed dividends that a private company is taken to pay under Division 7A is limited to its distributable surplus for that income year.

Being there is no distributable surplus, no deemed dividend is required.

Title: Payment of Company Tax
Content:
Companies are subject to full self-assessment which means that assessments (based upon the taxable income disclosed in returns) are deemed to be issued and the tax owing is due and payable upon lodgement of the Income Tax Return.

It is your responsibility to ensure that the amount of $xxx is paid to the Australian Taxation Office by the due date shown in this letter.

An income tax assessment notice will not be issued to the Company. Please use the enclosed payment advice and make your payment to the Australian Taxation Office by the due date.

Title: Personal Services Income
Content: The purpose of the personal services income (PSI) rules is to ensure that income derived from personal services is taxed in the hands of the individual who performs the work, regardless of whether the income is derived by an individual, partnership, company or trust. The PSI rules do not apply if the entity is treated as carrying on a personal services business (by passing certain tests). It appears that your Company is carrying on a personal services business as it passes the INSERT TEST. We have prepared your 2025 Tax Return on this basis.

Title: Potential Insolvent Trading
Content:
You will observe from the enclosed Financial Statements that this Company had a working capital deficiency at the end of the 202X financial year.

There is an obligation under the Corporations Law for the Directors to make a statement as to whether or not “there are reasonable grounds to believe that the company will be able to pay its debts as and when they fall due”.

If Directors knowingly make a false statement, they are in breach of the Corporations Law and may become personally liable for company debts. Likewise, if they make a statement that the company is unable to pay its debts but then continue to trade in that situation, they are also in breach of the Corporations Law.

If you consider, in view of the abovementioned deficiency, that your company is in this situation, it would appear that additional funds would need to be injected by the Directors/Shareholders in the form of subordinated loans or share capital. If by way of loans, the providers of such funds would then need to give an undertaking that they would not call up the loans unless all other creditors had been paid. The alternative is liquidation.

We strongly recommend that you consider our advice above, and then immediately contact our office if you would like to discuss this further.

Title: Employee Superannuation
Content:
During the 2025 financial year, it appears that Business Name has not paid the required 11.5% employer superannuation contribution for employees. The shortfall of superannuation contributions for the 2025 financial year is $shortfall amount.

The above payments were due to be paid by 28 July 2025. Please note that if your superannuation payment was made AFTER 28 July 2025, but was made PRIOR to 28 August 2025, the payment can still be made to a superannuation fund, however a Superannuation Guarantee Charge Statement must be completed and sent to the ATO along with a Super Guarantee Late Payment Offset Election to allow your payment to offset any liability created by the Superannuation Guarantee Charge Statement.

Alternatively, should payment be made after 28 August 2025, the payment must be made direct to the ATO and remitted with a Superannuation Guarantee Charge Statement. If payment was also made to a superannuation fund, you will not be eligible for an offset and the full Superannuation Guarantee Charge will still be liable.

If you didn’t pay these amounts by 28 July 2025, please contact our office immediately to determine the best outcome for your situation, as these issues can be quite complex! If needed, we can also prepare the appropriate documents for you to send to the ATO along with your payment.

Title: Extended Time for Lodgement
Content:
Because Client Name has tax payable, lodgement can be delayed should you desire. When the payment is due, however, relies upon the actual lodgement date of the return:

  • If lodged before 12 February 2026, payment will be due by 21 March 2026
  • If lodged between 13 February and 12 March 2026, payment will be due by 21 April 2026
  • If lodged between 13 March and 15 May 2026, payment will be due by 5 June 2026

As you can see, there are plenty of options that allow you to dictate when you pay the ATO and can allow for you to have the funds working for you a little longer rather than for the government!

Would you please advise when you would like us to lodge your return?

Title: HELP / HECS Balance
Content:
Your HECS balance is currently $xxx.

Changes to HECS / HELP Repayments in 2026

The minimum repayment threshold will change from $54,435 in 2024-25 to $67,000 in 2025-26.

This change will also introduce a system where HELP repayments are calculated only on the repayment income above the new $67,000 threshold rather than repayments being based on total annual income.

Note: repayment income differs from your taxable income and includes items such as reportable FBT, investment loss and reportable superannuation.

Title: Single Touch Payroll - Not Submitted / Finalisation
Content:
Single Touch Payroll (also called STP or one touch payroll) is an ATO initiative that requires all employers to report salaries and wages, PAYG withholding and superannuation electronically to the ATO each time they pay their employees, whether they pay one, two, twenty or more persons.

Each time you pay your employees you will be sending your STP data from Xero which includes: employee salary and wage information, pay as you go (PAYG) withholding and super information to the ATO. These will be year-to-date amounts. Corrections to an employee’s year-to-date amounts can be made in your next pay event or a separate update event.

At the end of the financial year you will provide a STP finalisation declaration to the ATO via Xero. You will not be required to provide employees with payment summaries for the information you report through STP. Income statements will replace payment summaries and these will be available to employees through myGov.

We have noted that end-of-year finalisation declaration through STP for the 2024-25 financial year has not yet been prepared and lodged with the ATO.

Please contact our office if you need assistance on preparing and lodging the STP Finalisation above.

Title: Additional Tax on Super for High Income Earners (Division 293)
Content:
Division 293 tax is an additional tax on super contributions which reduces the tax concession for individuals whose combined annual income and contributions are greater than $250,000.

If your annual income and super contributions exceeds $250,000, then you are taxed at 15% on either your contributions, or the amount that is over the threshold — whichever amount is lower.

We estimate you will need to pay $xxx on your superannuation contributions. The ATO will send you a Division 293 Tax Notice with their assessment of the amount payable after you lodge your annual Tax Return.

You can pay this Division 293 tax by:

  • Payment from your personal bank account, or
  • By electing to release money from any of your existing super balances by completing a Division 293 election form.

Title: Family Trust Election
Content:
We would recommend that the [Trust Name] make a Family Trust Election (“FTE”). A high level overview of Family Trust Elections is below.

What is an FTE?

An FTE makes the client’s trust a ‘family trust’ for taxation purposes, whereby distributions can only be made to eligible beneficiaries within a family group. The election must specify the year of income from which it is to take effect, and nominate one individual whose family group is to be taken into account.

Generally the following people are considered part of the family group:

  • the specified individual and their spouse;
  • any parent, grandparent, brother or sister of the specified individual or their spouse;
  • any nephew, niece or child of the specified individual or their spouse, including any lineal descendants; and
  • the spouse of any individual mentioned above.

Companies, partnerships and trusts may also be members of the family group, provided that the family members listed above are entitled to all the capital and income of the relevant entity.

Why Planning is Important

Where a trust makes an FTE, distributions may only be made to eligible beneficiaries who are within the family group. Any distributions outside the family group will be subject to Family Trust Distributions Tax calculated at the highest marginal rate applying to individuals plus the Medicare levy.

This means that considerable planning needs to be carried out when making an FTE to ensure suitability over the long term.

Why Make an FTE?

It may be worth considering making an FTE where:

  • the trust receives franked dividends;
  • the trust has losses;
  • the trust owns shares in a company with losses; or
  • the intention is to bring the trust within the family group of another trust.

Establishment of FTE for [NAME] Family Trust

A Family Trust Election has been setup for the [Trust Name] with [Name of Trustee/Specified Individual] as the specified individual.

Title: Tax Incentives for Early Stage Investors (ESIC)
Content:
Warning: overview current for 2025 year. Must be updated for individual circumstances.

The early stage investor tax offset is 20% of the total eligible investments in respect to the issue of qualifying shares. If you don’t meet the sophisticated investor test, the maximum early stage investor tax offset you can claim is $10,000, because your total annual investment in all qualifying ESICs cannot exceed $50,000.

Tax offsets directly reduce the amount of tax you have to pay, with each dollar of tax offset reducing your tax payable by the same amount. As the early stage investor tax offset is a non-refundable tax offset, it can reduce your amount of tax payable to zero, but it can’t result in a tax refund on its own.

Can a member of a trust or partnership be eligible for the early stage investor tax offset?

A member of a trust may be entitled to the early stage investor tax offset if all the following apply:

  • they are a member of the trust or partnership at the end of the income year;
  • the trust or partnership would be entitled to the tax offset for that income year if it were an individual investor; and
  • they are not a widely held company or a 100% subsidiary of a widely held company.

ESIC Offset for [xxx Family Trust]

On this basis, the ESIC Tax Offset for the purchase of new shares in [investing company name] has been calculated at $xxx, being 20% of your $xxx investment. This offset has been allocated to the beneficiaries in line with the distribution advice.

How CGT Applies to Qualifying Shares

Qualifying shares held for less than 12 months

If you continuously hold qualifying shares for less than 12 months before a CGT event happens to the shares, any capital gain you make from the event is not disregarded. However, you must disregard any capital loss you make from a CGT event that happens to the shares during this period.

Qualifying shares held for 12 months or more but less than 10 years

You can disregard a capital gain that you make from a CGT event happening to qualifying shares if you have held them continuously for 12 months or more but less than 10 years. However, you must disregard a capital loss that you make from a CGT event happening to the shares during this period.

Qualifying shares held for 10 years or more

If you have continuously held a qualifying share for 10 years, the first element of the cost base and reduced cost base for the share will become its market value on the tenth anniversary of the share being issued to you. This means that you will recognise any capital gains or losses that happen from this point in time.
